sections.content: Every vessel and shore station in the GMDSS is identified by a Maritime Mobile Service Identity (MMSI) — a unique 9-digit number programmed into the ship's DSC controller. When you press the distress button or send any DSC call, your MMSI is automatically included in the digital message. Unlike a voice call sign that you speak aloud, the MMSI is transmitted as data and decoded by the receiving equipment.

sections.title: Self-Powered on Installation
sections.content: The MMSI is programmed into the DSC equipment during installation and is automatically embedded in every DSC transmission. You never need to enter it manually before sending a call — the controller handles it.

The structure of a 9-digit MMSI reveals what kind of station it identifies. The key building block is the Maritime Identification Digit (MID) — a 3-digit country code assigned by the ITU. For example, MID 428 is assigned to Israel, 211 to Germany, and 311 to the United States. The position of the MID within the 9 digits, along with any leading prefix digits, tells you whether the MMSI belongs to a ship, a coast station, a group of vessels, an aircraft, or a navigational aid.

rows: MID in positions 1-3; most common type
Leading 0 + MID; used for fleet calls

sections.title: Reading an MMSI at a Glance
sections.content: If the first digit is non-zero, it is a ship station (MID starts at digit 1). If it begins with 00, it is a coast station. If it begins with a single 0, it is a group call. Prefixes 970, 972, and 974 indicate AIS-SART, MOB, and EPIRB-AIS devices respectively. Prefix 111 marks SAR aircraft, and 99 marks navigational aids.

Radio call signs serve a complementary role to the MMSI. A call sign is an alphanumeric identifier assigned by the vessel's flag state — for example, 4XAB2 (Israel) or DABC (Germany). The first one or two characters of a call sign identify the country of registration, following ITU allocations. While the MMSI is used exclusively in digital transmissions (DSC, AIS), the call sign is spoken during voice procedures and printed on the ship's radio licence.

sections.title: Call Sign Country Prefixes
sections.content: Call sign prefixes are allocated by the ITU in blocks. For example, prefixes starting with 4X are assigned to Israel, DA-DR to Germany, and K/N/W to the United States. The first one or two characters always identify the country.

sections.prompt: An MMSI beginning with 00 identifies which type of station?
sections.explanation: An MMSI with the prefix 00 followed by the MID identifies a coast station (e.g., 004281234). Ship stations have the MID in the first three positions with no leading zeros.

sections.prompt: The 3-digit country code embedded in every MMSI is called the Maritime Identification ___.
sections.explanation: The Maritime Identification Digit (MID) is a 3-digit code assigned by the ITU to each country. Its position within the 9-digit MMSI varies depending on the station type.

sections.prompt: Which MMSI prefix identifies an AIS-SART device?
sections.explanation: The prefix 970 identifies an AIS Search and Rescue Transponder (AIS-SART). Prefix 972 is for MOB devices, 974 for EPIRB-AIS, and 111 for SAR aircraft.

points: An MMSI is a unique 9-digit number automatically included in every DSC transmission.
The Maritime Identification Digit (MID) is a 3-digit ITU country code whose position in the MMSI indicates the station type.
Ship stations place the MID at positions 1-3; coast stations use prefix 00; group calls use prefix 0; SAR aircraft use 111; navigational aids use 99.
Call signs are alphanumeric identifiers used in voice procedures, with the first one or two characters identifying the country of registration.
